metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (and at minimum a smoke/unit test) for `scripts/harvest_gha.py` that verifies it correctly extracts labeled coverage snapshots from fixture run metadata and writes them to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`.
evidence: Files: 'scripts/harvest_gha.py — new: pull test.yml coverage totals + workflow-run outcomes into labeled snapshots'
Approach: 'we harvest the window as a seed'
Tests section: only mentions adapter extraction and recovery interval — no mention of harvest_gha.py
All three ACs reference test_gha_adapter.py, the portability guard, and the test.yml step — none reference harvest_gha.py
findings.finding: `scripts/harvest_gha.py` is named in the Files section and described in Approach as seeding history from the retention window, but no acceptance criterion covers it and the Tests section does not mention testing it — it is an orphan deliverable with no verification.
findings.location: Files section (`scripts/harvest_gha.py`); Tests section; Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)
scenarios: An implementer writes harvest_gha.py with a bug in its GHA API pagination or label logic; no test or AC catches it before merge
A reviewer cannot confirm the script is done because there is no acceptance bar defined for it
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '`scripts/harvest_gha.py` invoked against fixture run-metadata JSON produces correctly labeled NDJSON lines in the snapshot store (assert output contains expected coverage % and run-id label).' Add a corresponding fixture-based unit test in `tests/unit/metrics/test_gha_adapter.py` or a sibling file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC3's snapshot path from `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (the path hard-coded in `snapshot.py` and verified non-ignored by `test_snapshot_heldout.py`).
evidence: AC3: 'the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`'
`snapshot.py` line 23: `_SNAPSHOT_RELPATH = os.path.join('.rebar', 'metrics-snapshots.ndjson')`
`test_snapshot_heldout.py` line 79: `assert ignored('reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson')` — the `reports/` path is gitignored by design
findings.finding: AC3 names `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` as the snapshot store path, but the codebase hard-codes `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (`snapshot.py` line 23) and an existing test explicitly asserts the `reports/` path IS gitignored (and therefore non-persistent). The plan's AC3 path is factually wrong and an implementor following it would write to an ignored file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 / `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` line 23 / `tests/unit/metrics/test_snapshot_heldout.py` line 79
scenarios: An implementor follows AC3 literally and writes to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`, which is gitignored — coverage history never persists, silently defeating the plan's stated goal.
The workflow-lint/test in AC3 asserts the step targets the snapshot store, but if the path in the assertion matches `reports/`, the test passes while the actual persistence fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` with `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` in AC3 and in any workflow step template.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan an explicit description of how the `test.yml` snapshot-append step commits and pushes the updated `.ndjson` file, including the required `contents: write` permission elevation (or a PAT/app token) and the branch/commit strategy.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'persist coverage forward via the snapshot so history accrues durably'
`test.yml` line 24–25: `permissions:\n  contents: read`
No `contents: write` permission is present anywhere in `test.yml`; only `release.yml` and `reconcile-bridge*.yml` carry it
findings.finding: The plan's Approach asserts the `test.yml` step 'appends coverage to … the snapshot store' (implying a git commit/push of the updated `.ndjson` file), but `test.yml` currently has `permissions: contents: read`. Writing and committing a file requires `contents: write`; the plan never mentions this permission change, so the commit step would fail silently or be omitted.
findings.location: Approach section ('persist coverage forward via the snapshot') / `.github/workflows/test.yml` line 25 (`permissions: contents: read`)
scenarios: The new step tries to `git add .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson && git commit && git push` and fails with a 403/permission error because `contents: read` forbids pushes.
Alternatively, the step appends to the file but omits the commit entirely, making the persistence non-durable — the file is written to the ephemeral runner workspace and discarded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC3 to reference `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (not `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`) so the coverage-append step targets the git-tracked snapshot store.
evidence: src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py line 23: `_SNAPSHOT_RELPATH = os.path.join(".rebar", "metrics-snapshots.ndjson")`
.gitignore line 21: `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (explicitly un-ignored, i.e., tracked)
tests/unit/metrics/test_snapshot_heldout.py line 79: `assert ignored("reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson")` — reports/ path IS gitignored
AC3 text: 'appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`'
findings.finding: AC3 states the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`, but the established snapshot store path is `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (hardcoded in `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` line 23, confirmed by `.gitignore` line 21 and `tests/unit/metrics/test_snapshot_heldout.py` line 22). The `reports/` path is gitignored (asserted by `test_snapshot_heldout.py` line 79), meaning any data written there would not be tracked by git and would not accrue durably — directly undermining the plan's stated hardening goal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; also Approach section ('persist coverage forward via the snapshot so history accrues durably')
scenarios: The test.yml step writes to reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson; the data is gitignored and never committed; the durable going-forward series is never populated, defeating the hardening purpose.
The workflow-lint/test in AC3 asserts the step targets 'reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson' and passes, but the data silently evaporates after each CI run.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — a workflow-lint/test asserts the added step targets the snapshot store.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the persistence mechanism for the `test.yml` coverage-append step: either elevate the `test` job's permissions to `contents: write` and add a `git commit --allow-empty + git push` step, or use a separate commit-and-push action (e.g. `stefanzweifel/git-auto-commit-action`) — and document why the matrix concurrency / `cancel-in-progress: true` race is safe (e.g. only one matrix leg writes, or an append-only file merge strategy is used).
evidence: Plan states: 'persist coverage forward via the snapshot so history accrues durably'
Existing test.yml sets `permissions: contents: read` at workflow level — a write+commit+push step would need `contents: write`
`snapshot.py` writes to a file path; that file is in the git-tracked workspace and is lost at runner teardown unless committed
No commit, git-push, or artifact-upload step is mentioned for the appended line
findings.finding: The `test.yml` step that appends coverage to the snapshot store has no persistence mechanism: the workflow declares `permissions: contents: read` (cannot commit+push), and the plan never specifies how the appended NDJSON line survives beyond the ephemeral runner — making the entire 'going-forward hardening' goal a no-op.
findings.location: Approach section ('persist coverage forward via the snapshot') / AC3 / .github/workflows/test.yml line 25 (`permissions: contents: read`)
scenarios: Implementer adds the append step but the file write silently succeeds then disappears — coverage history never accumulates
Implementer adds a commit+push sub-step but hits a 403 because `contents: read` blocks it

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC3 to reference `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (the actual durable snapshot store path, per `snapshot.py` and the gitignore contract) instead of `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`.
evidence: `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` line 23: `_SNAPSHOT_RELPATH = os.path.join(".rebar", "metrics-snapshots.ndjson")`
`tests/unit/metrics/test_snapshot_heldout.py` line 79: `assert ignored("reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson")` — confirms this path is explicitly gitignored
AC3 literally names `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` as the target
findings.finding: AC3 references `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` as the target snapshot store, but the existing store path — per `snapshot.py` (`_SNAPSHOT_RELPATH`) and all existing tests — is `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`; `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is the intentionally gitignored path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`'
scenarios: Implementer follows AC3 literally and writes to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`, which is gitignored — the snapshot is never committed and the durability goal is defeated
AC3 workflow-lint test asserts the step targets `reports/...` and passes even though the write goes to the wrong (ignored) path
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — a workflow-lint/test asserts the added step targets the snapshot store.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion for `scripts/harvest_gha.py` that provides a concrete proving command (e.g., a dry-run or fixture-driven invocation) verifying the script parses fixture run-metadata and emits labeled ndjson records.
evidence: 'scripts/harvest_gha.py — new: pull test.yml coverage totals + workflow-run outcomes into labeled snapshots' is a listed file with no corresponding AC
AC1 tests the adapter class; AC3 tests the test.yml step; no AC says anything about harvest_gha.py
findings.finding: `scripts/harvest_gha.py` is listed as a deliverable in the Files section but has no acceptance criterion that maps to it — AC1 covers the adapter unit tests, AC2 covers portability, and AC3 covers `test.yml`; the union of ACs never verifies that the harvest script works at all.
findings.location: Files — `scripts/harvest_gha.py`; Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: The harvest script is never run; CI stays green via the other ACs; the harvest capability is silently undelivered
The script has a broken argument or import that would fail at runtime, but no AC catches it

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC/test for flake/recheck rate extraction, or remove it from the What section scope to keep the declared scope and acceptance bar consistent.
evidence: What: 'CI-health (status, duration, red->green recovery, flake/recheck rate)'
AC1 verifies coverage % and red->green recovery only
AC2 verifies portability
AC3 verifies test.yml step — none mention flake/recheck rate
findings.finding: 'flake/recheck rate' is listed as a harvested metric in the What section but appears in no acceptance criterion and no test — it is an orphan concept with no verification path.
findings.location: What section ('CI-health (status, duration, red->green recovery, flake/recheck rate)')
scenarios: Implementer skips flake-rate computation entirely; no AC fails
Implementer implements it incorrectly; no test catches it

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC3 to target `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (the parent-mandated, git-tracked snapshot store path), not `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (gitignored; would silently drop every snapshot).
evidence: Leaf AC3: 'the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — a workflow-lint/test asserts the added step targets the snapshot store.'

Parent Approach (Snapshot store path): 'The going-forward snapshot series (3275) is committed to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — a git-tracked path in the rebar store — NOT under `reports/`, which is gitignored (`.gitignore:74`) and would silently drop every snapshot.'
Codebase corroboration: `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py:23` sets `_SNAPSHOT_RELPATH = os.path.join(".rebar", "metrics-snapshots.ndjson")`
The parent explicitly names the wrong path (`reports/`) and explains the failure mode, making this a deliberate authoritative specification the leaf contradicts.
findings.finding: The leaf's AC3 targets `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` as the snapshot store path, but the parent epic explicitly mandates `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` and warns that `reports/` is gitignored and 'would silently drop every snapshot'. The leaf contradicts a parent-specified deliverable path, and per G7 the parent wins.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`'
scenarios: An implementer follows the leaf's AC3 literally, writes the snapshot to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`, and every coverage snapshot is silently lost to gitignore — exactly the failure mode the parent flagged.

The workflow-lint test passes (step targets `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` as stated) but the integration test for the snapshot store reads from `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` and finds nothing, causing a silent data-loss bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — a workflow-lint/test asserts the added step targets the canonical snapshot store (`.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`, not `reports/`).' This aligns with the parent's Snapshot store path note and the existing `snapshot.py` implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `test.yml` coverage-append step persists its write across runs: either (a) add `permissions: contents: write` to the job/workflow and describe the git commit+push step, or (b) use a different persistence mechanism (e.g., upload-artifact + a scheduled consolidation job) and document why `contents: read` suffices for it — the plan currently names a write outcome under a read-only permission.
evidence: test.yml line 24-25: `permissions:\n  contents: read`
Plan Approach: 'The test.yml edit adds only a step under the existing push/PR triggers'
Plan AC3: 'the test.yml change appends coverage to reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson'
Appending to a tracked file and persisting it across runs requires git commit+push, which requires contents: write — CONTRADICTED by the current workflow permission
reconcile-bridge-canary.yml and jira-sync-setup.md both show that push-back operations explicitly require contents: write
findings.finding: The `test.yml` workflow declares `permissions: contents: read` at the workflow level, but the plan's AC3 requires a step that appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` and commits/pushes it — which requires `permissions: contents: write`. The plan never mentions elevating this permission, making the going-forward snapshot-hardening step structurally impossible as described.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 / .github/workflows/test.yml (permissions block, line 24–25) / Approach section ('The test.yml edit adds only a step')
scenarios: The new coverage-append step runs in CI, writes the file locally, then fails silently or with a permission error when attempting to commit/push — coverage is never durably persisted.
If the step only writes locally without committing, the 'going-forward hardening' goal is not achieved (the file is ephemeral to the runner).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `permissions` block to the new step's job (or a dedicated job) granting `contents: write`, and add an explicit 'git add / git commit / git push' step. Alternatively, reframe the persistence as an artifact upload and schedule a separate consolidation job — but the plan must name which mechanism it uses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `actions: read` to the `test.yml` job (or the new step's job) permissions and document this requirement in the plan's auth/preconditions — the current `contents: read`-only grant cannot call the Actions run-history API.
evidence: test.yml line 24-25: only `contents: read` granted
.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge-canary.yml: `actions: read     # read reconcile-bridge.yml run history` — explicitly shows this permission is required to read Actions run history
.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml line 38: `actions: read   # workflow-conclusion queries this run's jobs`
Plan never mentions `actions: read` permission
Evidence class: Contradicted — the existing workflow permission set is insufficient for the named operation
findings.finding: Reading GitHub Actions run history via `gh run list` (or `gh api /repos/.../actions/runs`) from within a CI job requires the `actions: read` permission on `GITHUB_TOKEN`, but `test.yml` only grants `contents: read`. The plan never mentions this additional permission scope, so the harvest script cannot call the Actions API from within the workflow.
findings.location: Approach section ('CI-health in the run metadata') / .github/workflows/test.yml permissions block
scenarios: The harvest step calls `gh run list` inside the `test.yml` job and receives a 403 because `GITHUB_TOKEN` lacks `actions: read`, silently skipping all CI-health data.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the relevant job's permissions block: `actions: read  # required by harvest_gha.py to call gh run list / Actions API`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether 'snapshot label' means a key inside the `record` dict passed to `write_snapshot`, or whether the snapshot API needs a new `label` parameter — and update the AC accordingly.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'output carries the snapshot label'
`write_snapshot(record: dict, *, repo_root, ts: str)` — signature has no `label` parameter
The stored line format is `{"ts": ts, "record": record}` — no label field at the envelope level
findings.finding: The Approach section claims 'output carries the snapshot label', but `write_snapshot()` in `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` accepts only `record` (dict) and `ts` (str) — there is no 'label' parameter or field in the existing snapshot API. The plan does not specify whether the label is embedded in the record dict or requires an API extension.
findings.location: Approach section ('output carries the snapshot label') / `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` lines 39–47
scenarios: An implementor assumes `write_snapshot` supports a label argument and writes code that fails at runtime with an unexpected keyword argument.
The adapter silently embeds the label inside `record` with an undocumented key, making the AC test ('output carries the snapshot label') ambiguous to verify.
findings.suggested_fix: If the label is record-level, specify the key name (e.g., `{"source": "gha", "coverage_pct": 84.2, ...}`) so the AC test assertion is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC2 which module(s) constitute 'the core command' (e.g., `rebar.metrics.registry` or the CLI entry point) so the portability-guard test has a concrete, greppable target.
evidence: AC2 text: 'a unit test asserts the core command does not import the GHA adapter'
No section of the plan identifies which Python module or CLI entrypoint is 'the core command' (candidates include rebar.__main__, rebar._cli, or the metrics registry — all unnamed)
findings.finding: AC2 requires a unit test asserting 'the core command does not import the GHA adapter', but the plan never names which module or entrypoint constitutes 'the core command'. Without a concrete target, an implementer cannot write a deterministic import-guard test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
scenarios: An implementer writes the portability-guard test targeting the wrong entry point; the guard passes vacuously while a genuine import leak in the real core command goes undetected.
A reviewer cannot verify AC2 is satisfied without knowing which module to inspect.
findings.suggested_fix: Example: 'a unit test asserts that `rebar.metrics.registry` (and/or `rebar._cli`) does not transitively import `rebar.metrics.adapters.github_actions`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what 'snapshot label' means: specify the dict key name, its expected value (e.g., `record['source'] == 'gha'`), and whether it is a field inside the `record` dict or a wrapper field added by the adapter.
evidence: Plan Tests: 'output carries the snapshot label'
snapshot.py write_snapshot signature: `write_snapshot(record: dict, *, repo_root, ts: str)` — no 'label' parameter
ABSENCE: 'snapshot label' appears nowhere in registry.py, snapshot.py, or any existing test file
findings.finding: The Tests section states 'output carries the snapshot label' but 'snapshot label' is never defined — it is not a field in snapshot.py's write_snapshot API (which takes `record: dict` and `ts: str`), not a key in REGISTRY MetricSpec, and not explained anywhere in the plan. An implementer cannot know what to assert.
findings.location: Tests section: 'output carries the snapshot label'
scenarios: Implementer invents a label field (e.g., record['label'] = 'gha_coverage') that is never tested or validated against a spec
AC1 assertion 'output carries the snapshot label' cannot be written as a unit test without a definition of what the label is or where it lives

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of the `test.yml` snapshot-write step: specify the minimum required permission (e.g., `permissions: contents: write`), name the credential used, and add an explicit fork-PR exclusion guard (e.g., `if: github.event_name == 'push' || github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name == github.repository`) so lower-trust fork PRs cannot reach the write path.
evidence: Plan states the step runs 'under the existing push/PR triggers' — PR triggers can be fired by external contributors via fork PRs
Plan states the step 'appends coverage to reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson' but is silent on which credential performs this write and what its scope is

No GITHUB_TOKEN permission declaration (e.g., `permissions: contents: write`) or fork-PR exclusion guard (`if: github.event_name != 'pull_request' || github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name == github.repository`) appears anywhere in the plan
ABSENCE: the plan introduces a new write-to-persistent-store operation in CI but provides no access-level declaration for it
findings.finding: The new `.github/workflows/test.yml` step that writes coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` does not declare its credential posture: which credential performs the write (GITHUB_TOKEN with `contents: write`? A PAT?), what minimum permission scope that credential carries, or whether fork-PR contexts are explicitly excluded from triggering the write (a lower-trust actor opening a fork PR could otherwise influence what gets committed to the snapshot store).
findings.location: .github/workflows/test.yml — 'append the current coverage % to the snapshot store on each run' step / AC line 3
scenarios: An external contributor opens a fork PR; the step fires in the fork's sandboxed context but the credential and write-path behaviour are undefined — if a PAT with write access were accidentally used, the untrusted fork could poison the snapshot store; even with GITHUB_TOKEN the silent failure mode is undocumented
The GITHUB_TOKEN defaults to read-only on newer GitHub repo settings; the write silently fails with no plan-specified fallback, leaving the 'going-forward hardening' goal silently broken
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's `.github/workflows/test.yml` section, add: (1) a job-level `permissions: contents: write` (scoped to the snapshot step only, or via a dedicated job); (2) a step condition `if: github.event_name == 'push' || (github.event_name == 'pull_request' && github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name == github.repository)` to exclude fork PRs; (3) a note that GITHUB_TOKEN is used (no PAT), so no secret-manager entry is needed beyond the built-in token.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'a workflow-lint/test' in AC3 with a concrete proving command naming the tool and/or test file (e.g., `yq e '.jobs.test.steps[].run' .github/workflows/test.yml | grep -q metrics-snapshots.ndjson`).
evidence: 'a workflow-lint/test asserts the added step targets the snapshot store' — 'workflow-lint/test' is not a named tool or file
No proving command (e.g., `actionlint .github/workflows/test.yml` or `pytest tests/unit/test_workflow_step.py`) is given
findings.finding: AC3's proving mechanism ('a workflow-lint/test asserts the added step targets the snapshot store') names no concrete tool, command, or test file — an executor cannot know whether to write a Python test, run `actionlint`, use `yq` on the YAML, or something else.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3
scenarios: Executor writes any test that trivially passes without actually checking the step's target path
Two executors independently implement two incompatible proof approaches, neither of which the reviewer expects
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3's verification phrase with: `yq e '[.jobs[].steps[].run // ""] | join("\n")' .github/workflows/test.yml | grep -qF 'reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the NDJSON record schema for the coverage snapshot entry (key names, types, required fields) so AC3 and the unit test in AC1 can assert a concrete shape rather than just store reachability.
evidence: AC3: 'a workflow-lint/test asserts the added step targets the snapshot store' — schema of appended record is entirely absent
snapshot.py write_snapshot takes `record: dict` with no enforced shape
Existing usage in test_snapshot.py uses `{"coverage_pct": 91.2}` but the plan never adopts or references this key
findings.finding: The NDJSON record schema written to the snapshot store by the `test.yml` coverage step is never specified. The existing `write_snapshot` API is key-agnostic, so without a declared dict shape (e.g., `{"coverage_pct": 84.2, "run_id": "...", "source": "gha"}`), AC3 ('a workflow-lint/test asserts the added step targets the snapshot store') cannot be mechanically verified, and the harvest script and the workflow step will likely produce incompatible record shapes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 / .github/workflows/test.yml / scripts/harvest_gha.py
scenarios: test.yml step writes `{"cov": 84.2}`, harvest script writes `{"coverage_pct": 84.2}` — both pass AC3 superficially but produce an incoherent time series
The workflow-lint test in AC3 can only assert presence of a step, not correctness of the record shape, without a schema
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Record shape' subsection to Approach: e.g., `{"coverage_pct": <float>, "run_id": <str>, "workflow": "test.yml", "source": "gha"}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the ndjson record schema (field names, types, and an example record) for the coverage-append step in `test.yml`, and extend AC3 to assert the appended record matches that schema.
evidence: 'appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`' — no field names, types, or example record are given
AC3 only checks that the step *targets* the snapshot store, not that the record format is correct
The Tests section mentions 'output carries the snapshot label' but never defines what a label is or its format
findings.finding: The ndjson record schema/format for the `test.yml` coverage-append step is never specified — an executor cannot implement the step without inventing a schema, and AC3 cannot be verified without knowing what a valid append looks like.
findings.location: Files — `.github/workflows/test.yml`; Acceptance Criteria — AC3
scenarios: Executor writes a step that appends an ad-hoc JSON shape incompatible with the rest of the snapshot store
AC3 is satisfied by a trivially wrong append (e.g., a bare string) because the format is unchecked
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line example to the plan, e.g.: `{"label": "ci/coverage", "run_id": "<GHA_RUN_ID>", "sha": "<SHA>", "coverage_pct": 84.2, "ts": "<ISO8601>"}` and update AC3 to `jq -e '.coverage_pct | type == "number"' <(tail -1 reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the log-parsing pattern (grep anchor for coverage TOTAL line) configurable or at minimum document the expected format and add a parse-failure signal when no match is found.
evidence: Plan asserts a fixed grep pattern for log parsing without making the pattern configurable
No mention of what happens if the pattern doesn't match (silent zero? exception?)
pytest-cov output format has changed between major versions; different reporters produce different TOTAL line formats
findings.finding: The log-parsing anchor ('word-boundary grep for the coverage TOTAL line') is a hardcoded heuristic with no configurable pattern, and is likely to break silently if pytest-cov output format changes or a non-default reporter is used.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Log parsing is anchored (word-boundary grep for the coverage TOTAL line)'
scenarios: A pytest-cov upgrade changes 'TOTAL' line formatting, silently producing 0% or crashing the harvest
A project using a custom coverage reporter never populates the snapshot
findings.suggested_fix: Allow the pattern to be set via an environment variable or config key (e.g. GHA_COVERAGE_PATTERN); in the adapter, raise/log a clear error when the pattern matches nothing rather than silently emitting 0 or None.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and test non-happy-path behaviour in `harvest_gha.py`: at minimum, `gh` auth failure (exit non-zero), expired/missing run logs, and absent TOTAL line — each should produce a distinct, actionable error or warning rather than silent failure.
evidence: "the adapter extracts the coverage % from a fixture log tail" — tests cover only the happy path (fixture log always contains the TOTAL line)

No AC or test specifies behaviour when `gh` returns a non-zero exit code, when a run log has already expired beyond the retention window, or when the coverage line is absent (e.g., test suite failed before coverage reporting)
The plan names no timeout, retry, or partial-data handling for the API harvest loop
findings.finding: The `scripts/harvest_gha.py` script has no specified error handling for non-happy-path states: `gh` not authenticated, API rate-limit exhausted, the retention window already expired for target runs, or no TOTAL coverage line present in a log.
findings.location: Approach — 'harvest the window as a seed'; Tests — 'a fixture `test.yml` log yields the parsed coverage %'; Acceptance Criteria AC1
scenarios: A developer runs `harvest_gha.py` without a valid `gh` auth token → script exits with an unhandled exception or silent empty output, giving no actionable error message
All runs in the retention window pre-date the current invocation by more than the GHA log-retention period → the script silently produces no snapshots and the user does not know why
A run log lacks the TOTAL line (e.g., test suite crashed) → the adapter either raises an unhandled parse error or silently skips the entry; neither outcome is specified or tested

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for a missing or malformed TOTAL line in the log (e.g., return `None` / skip the snapshot write / raise a named exception), and add a failure-path fixture to AC1.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'Log parsing is anchored (word-boundary grep for the coverage TOTAL line)' — no error handling specified
ABSENCE: no fallback, exception type, sentinel return value, or 'raise on missing' policy stated anywhere in the plan
AC1 only tests the happy path ('a fixture test.yml log yields the parsed coverage %')
findings.finding: The Approach states log parsing is 'anchored (word-boundary grep for the coverage TOTAL line)' but specifies no fallback for the case where the TOTAL line is absent, malformed, or matches multiple lines. Without a defined fallback, the adapter's behavior on a corrupt or unexpected log format is implementation-defined, making AC1's `assert pct == 84.2` untestable for the error path.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Log parsing is anchored (word-boundary grep for the coverage TOTAL line)'
scenarios: The coverage report format changes (e.g., pytest-cov updates its TOTAL line format); the adapter silently returns None or raises — behavior is undefined
A log is truncated mid-run; grep matches zero lines — adapter has no specified behavior

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling strategy for gh/API failures in the adapter (at minimum: timeout, logged error, and non-zero exit that does not block core CI), and ensure the snapshot-append step is idempotent (e.g. deduplicate by run-id before writing).
evidence: Plan describes harvesting from 'gh'/the Actions API but mentions no retry, backoff, timeout, or circuit-breaker
No mention of what happens when gh auth fails, the API rate-limits, or a log fetch times out
harvest_gha.py writes to snapshot store with no mention of idempotency or failure handling
findings.finding: The plan adds external-system calls (gh CLI, Actions API) and file writes with no error handling, retry, or graceful-degradation strategy specified for any failure mode.
findings.location: Files section — github_actions.py, scripts/harvest_gha.py; Approach section
scenarios: gh CLI is unauthenticated or rate-limited; harvest_gha.py crashes mid-write, leaving a corrupt NDJSON entry
The Actions API is slow; harvest_gha.py hangs indefinitely blocking CI
CI re-runs the test.yml step twice; duplicate coverage entries are appended to the snapshot
findings.suggested_fix: In github_actions.py, wrap gh/API calls with a try/except that logs the error and returns an empty/None result rather than raising; in the test.yml step, set `continue-on-error: true` so a harvest failure doesn't break the core CI run; in harvest_gha.py, check for existing run-id before appending to avoid duplicates on re-runs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact `gh`/Actions API call used to retrieve run logs (confirming zip-vs-plaintext response, authentication scope needed, and availability in the CI runner environment) and document the verified call in the plan before committing to the log-scraping approach.
evidence: Plan states adapter is 'over gh/the Actions API' without specifying which gh subcommand or API endpoint is used
GitHub Actions log download API (GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/runs/{run_id}/logs) returns a redirect to a zip archive, not plain text — requires unzip to access individual job logs
No mention of authentication requirements for gh in the CI step or harvest script
Retention window behavior under the zip-archive response is not addressed
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the `gh` CLI can download per-job log tails to feed the CI-health harvest, but does not verify that `gh` is available and the specific `gh api /runs/{id}/logs` endpoint returns parseable content (logs are returned as a zip archive, not plain text) within the retention window. This is an unverified capability assertion for the external tool integration.
findings.location: Approach section — 'coverage is in the `test.yml` run-log tail'; Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/github_actions.py` — new isolated adapter over `gh`/the Actions API'
scenarios: harvest_gha.py assumes `gh run view --log` or similar and the actual API returns a zip redirect, causing silent parse failure
The `gh` CLI is not pre-installed on self-hosted or non-ubuntu runners
GITHUB_TOKEN permissions are insufficient for the log-download endpoint

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `gh run list --help` and `gh run view --help` flag-by-flag: confirm the exact `--json` field names for run timing/duration, and confirm whether `gh run view --log` supports a `--job` or step-level filter flag; add the verified flag names explicitly to the plan's Approach section.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'CI-health in the run metadata' — implies `gh run list --json <fields>` or `gh api /repos/.../actions/runs`
Plan Approach: 'coverage is in the test.yml run-log tail' — implies `gh run view <id> --log` with some job/step filter
docs/releasing.md uses `gh run list --json databaseId` confirming the `--json` flag exists, but does not confirm timing/duration fields
docs/jira-sync-setup.md uses `gh run view <databaseId> --log` confirming the `--log` flag exists, but does not confirm `--job` or step-level filter flags
Evidence class: Partially-verified (subcommands exist, specific field names and filter flags unconfirmed)
findings.finding: The `gh run list` and `gh run view --log` subcommands are used for harvesting run metadata and log content, but the specific `--json` field names needed for duration/timing (e.g., `durationMs`, `createdAt`, `updatedAt`) and the job/step filter flags needed for `--log` to isolate the coverage line are unverified against actual `gh` CLI `--help` output. Flag-level mismatches here cause silent runtime failures.
findings.location: Approach section ('coverage is in the test.yml run-log tail and CI-health in the run metadata') / Files section (`scripts/harvest_gha.py`, `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/github_actions.py`)
scenarios: `gh run list --json durationMs` fails because the field is named differently (e.g., it must be computed from `createdAt`+`updatedAt`), causing the harvest script to silently emit null durations.
`gh run view <id> --log` without a job filter emits all jobs' logs, and the grep for the coverage TOTAL line matches the wrong job's output when multiple jobs run coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the `source` enum value the GHA adapter will use (e.g., add `"ci"` to `SOURCES` in `registry.py`, or confirm it uses `"snapshot"`) and specify the `confidence` value — both must be drawn from the closed vocabularies defined in `registry.py`.
evidence: registry.py line 38: `SOURCES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({"structural", "git", "sidecar", "snapshot", "backfill_classified"})`
Plan Approach: 'the CI source is an isolated adapter the core command never imports' — implies a new source provenance, but its vocabulary entry is absent
ABSENCE: no mention of which SOURCES/CONFIDENCES enum value the GHA adapter assigns to its MetricSpec or MetricValue
findings.finding: The GHA adapter is described as writing output to labeled snapshots and registering CI-health metrics, but the plan never declares which `source` value from the closed SOURCES vocabulary (`{structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}`) GHA adapter records will carry. 'gha' or 'ci' are not in the frozenset; using an undefined value would cause `is_authoritative()` to silently return False, misclassifying the signal.
findings.location: Approach section / src/rebar/metrics/registry.py SOURCES frozenset
scenarios: An implementer picks an ad-hoc source string like 'gha' or 'ci_actions'; is_authoritative() returns False silently, metrics are excluded from authoritative series without warning
A MetricSpec registered with source='gha' passes the registry uniqueness check but fails any downstream source-validation gate
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Approach or AC: 'The GHA adapter registers its MetricSpecs with source="ci" (add to SOURCES) and confidence="high".' Update registry.py SOURCES/AUTHORITATIVE_SOURCES accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability for harvest failures: at minimum, log a structured warning (with run-id and error type) when any gh/API call or snapshot write fails, so failures are visible in CI logs.
evidence: No mention of logging, metrics, or alerting for harvest failures anywhere in the plan
Snapshot store is described as 'durable' but there is no signal when it stops being populated
findings.finding: New failure points in the adapter (API calls, log fetches, snapshot writes) are not instrumented with any logging, metric, or alert, leaving operators unable to detect silent harvest failures.
findings.location: Files section — github_actions.py, scripts/harvest_gha.py
scenarios: API auth silently expires; the snapshot store stops receiving new entries; no one notices until metrics are stale by weeks

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a concurrency group (or equivalent serialisation) for the snapshot-append step in `test.yml`, specify an idempotency key (e.g., run ID deduplication), and add a retention/pruning policy for `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` to prevent unbounded accumulation.
evidence: "the `test.yml` change appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`" — append-only with no pruning policy means unbounded growth
GitHub Actions allows concurrent workflow runs by default; two runs reaching the append step simultaneously will each read-then-write without coordination, risking lost or doubled entries
If a run is re-triggered ('Re-run jobs'), the step appends a duplicate coverage entry for the same run; no deduplication or idempotency guard is specified
No RETIRE/cleanup path is named anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The NDJSON snapshot file (`reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`) has no specified retirement/pruning path, is not idempotent under workflow re-runs, and has a concurrent-write race when multiple GitHub Actions runs (e.g., simultaneous PR + push) both reach the append step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `test.yml` change appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`; Approach — 'persist coverage forward via the snapshot'
scenarios: Two open PRs both push simultaneously → both runs append to the same file in their respective checkouts, then both commit+push → the second push is rejected (merge conflict on the snapshot file) or one entry is silently dropped, depending on commit strategy
A developer re-runs a failed workflow → the coverage entry for that run is written twice, inflating run counts and corrupting recovery-interval calculations downstream
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `concurrency: group: snapshot-append, cancel-in-progress: false` block to the `test.yml` job (or use a queue-based serialisation); write entries keyed by `github.run_id` and deduplicate on read; document a rolling-window pruning policy (e.g., keep last 90 days) with a periodic cleanup job or a trim step in `harvest_gha.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `adapters/` sub-namespace is justified now (>=2 other concrete CI adapters in scope) or whether a flat `src/rebar/metrics/github_actions.py` satisfies the isolation requirement without premature hierarchy.
evidence: List of `src/rebar/metrics/` contains only flat .py files: blame.py, event_metrics.py, git_metrics.py, registry.py, sidecar_metrics.py, snapshot.py — no `adapters/` sub-directory exists.
Plan states justification as 'a parallel adapter could target another CI' — a hypothetical with no current requirement.
Rule-of-Three: only 1 adapter planned, with 0 existing call-sites for the sub-namespace pattern in `src/rebar/metrics/`.
findings.finding: The plan places the single GHA adapter under a new `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` sub-namespace, justified by 'a parallel adapter could target another CI' — a hypothetical future use, not a current requirement. All three existing metrics modules (git_metrics.py, event_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py) are flat files directly under `src/rebar/metrics/`; no `adapters/` sub-namespace exists. Introducing a directory hierarchy for a single file violates Rule-of-Three and YAGNI, adding structural complexity without a current done-definition need.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/github_actions.py`; Approach section: 'a parallel adapter could target another CI'
scenarios: A developer adding a second metrics module later must decide whether to place it under `adapters/` (if it's CI-source-specific) or at root level, with no ADR or convention to guide them — the premature hierarchy creates ambiguity.
The extra directory level adds an `__init__.py` and import path (`rebar.metrics.adapters.github_actions`) for zero current benefit beyond what `rebar.metrics.github_actions` would provide.
findings.suggested_fix: Place the adapter as `src/rebar/metrics/github_actions.py` (flat, like the other three metrics modules); the portability guard (core never imports it) holds regardless of directory depth. Promote to an `adapters/` sub-namespace only when a second CI backend is actually being built.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` to the Files section so the new `adapters/` package is importable.
evidence: list_directory('src/rebar/metrics') shows: __init__.py, blame.py, event_metrics.py, git_metrics.py, registry.py, sidecar_metrics.py, snapshot.py — no 'adapters/' subdirectory
Plan Files section lists only 'src/rebar/metrics/adapters/github_actions.py'; no __init__.py for the new adapters/ package is listed
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/github_actions.py` as a new file, but the parent directory `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` does not exist and no `__init__.py` for it is listed in the Files section. Creating `github_actions.py` inside a new package directory requires also creating the package init file; omitting it leaves the adapter unimportable as a module.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/github_actions.py`
scenarios: An implementer creates github_actions.py under a new adapters/ dir without an __init__.py; Python cannot find it via `from rebar.metrics.adapters import github_actions`.
The portability-guard unit test in AC2 imports the adapter to verify it is NOT imported by core — that import also fails without the __init__.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` (empty or with a package docstring) to the Files list alongside `github_actions.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the matrix-concurrency race for the snapshot-write step: either restrict the write to a single designated matrix leg (e.g. `if: matrix.os == 'ubuntu-latest' && matrix.python-version == '3.12'`) or move the append step to a separate single-runner post-job, and note this constraint in the plan.
evidence: test.yml matrix: `os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest]` × `python-version: [3.11, 3.12, 3.13]` — up to 4 concurrent legs
test.yml concurrency: `cancel-in-progress: true` — legs can be cancelled mid-run
Plan says 'appends coverage to … snapshot' with no qualifier on which matrix leg writes
No atomicity or single-writer constraint stated in the plan
findings.finding: The `test` job matrix runs multiple OS/Python combinations concurrently with `cancel-in-progress: true`; if more than one matrix leg writes and commits the snapshot (required for persistence), concurrent commits to the same file create a race/ordering hazard that the plan does not acknowledge or address.
findings.location: Approach section / .github/workflows/test.yml lines 27-29 (concurrency group with cancel-in-progress:true, matrix strategy)
scenarios: Two matrix legs both reach the append step at the same time; the second git-push fails with 'non-fast-forward' error, dropping a coverage data point
A leg is cancelled mid-write, leaving a partial NDJSON line in the snapshot file
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `if: matrix.os == 'ubuntu-latest' && matrix.python-version == '3.12'` guard to the coverage-append step so exactly one leg performs the write per run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate `--cov-report=json` (or `coverage json`) as a structured prior-art alternative to log-tail grep before committing to the word-boundary parsing approach; document why log scraping is preferred if it is retained.
evidence: Plan states: 'Log parsing is anchored (word-boundary grep for the coverage TOTAL line)'
test.yml already runs: --cov=rebar --cov-report=term-missing:skip-covered (line 393) — adding --cov-report=json would provide structured output
coverage.py supports `coverage json` producing machine-readable totals (prior art in same toolchain)
No mention of structured alternatives (--cov-report=json, coverage.json artifact, Codecov, Coveralls) in plan
findings.finding: The plan proposes word-boundary grep on GHA log tails to extract the coverage `TOTAL` line, but `pytest-cov` (already in use in `test.yml`) can emit `--cov-report=json` and `coverage json` can post-process the `.coverage` data file — both producing a stable, structured numeric total without log parsing. Established tools like Codecov/Coveralls also do exactly this harvest-and-persist workflow. The plan does not mention any of these prior-art alternatives, risking reinvention of less-reliable log scraping when structured outputs already exist in the same runner environment.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Log parsing is anchored (word-boundary grep for the coverage TOTAL line)'
scenarios: Term-missing output format changes across pytest-cov versions, breaking the grep anchor silently
Multi-line terminal wrapping on narrow columns in CI produces a differently formatted TOTAL line
A contributor adds --cov-report=xml in a later PR and the log-tail structure shifts
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--cov-report=json` to the pytest invocation in `test.yml`; the harvest step then reads `coverage.json` (`.totals.percent_covered`) instead of grepping log tails. This eliminates the format-fragility risk and does not require `gh` log download.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a deduplication/idempotency strategy for `scripts/harvest_gha.py` (e.g., write run-ID as a key field and skip-on-exists before appending each entry) so the script is safely re-runnable without producing duplicate NDJSON records.
evidence: ABSENCE: the plan contains no deduplication key, upsert logic, or run-ID uniqueness check for NDJSON entries
'harvest the window as a seed' implies a one-shot operation with no re-run safety
findings.finding: The harvest script (`scripts/harvest_gha.py`) performs a one-shot bulk write of all in-window run history into `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` with no idempotency guard. Re-running the script (e.g., on failure and retry) will double-append duplicate snapshot entries, corrupting the store.
findings.location: scripts/harvest_gha.py (Approach section: 'harvest the window as a seed')
scenarios: Harvest script fails mid-run (network error, API rate-limit) and is re-run — already-written entries are duplicated
Operator reruns harvest after a partial CI outage — metrics dashboards show inflated counts/averages
findings.suggested_fix: Before appending each harvested run, read existing entries' run-IDs into a set and skip any run already present. Alternatively, write to a temp file and atomic-rename only after verifying no duplicates, and document the expected re-run behavior in the script's header.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a back-out path for the harvest script: either (a) take a git-committed snapshot of the file before running (so `git checkout` restores it), or (b) write all entries to a staging file first and atomic-rename/append only on full success, and document the rollback procedure explicitly in the plan.
evidence: ABSENCE: the plan names no backup step, no atomic file swap, and no post-failure schema-state assertion for the NDJSON file
ABSENCE: no mention of what 'rollback' means for this file (truncate to pre-harvest length? restore from git? delete new entries?)
findings.finding: The plan specifies no rollback / back-out path for `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` if `scripts/harvest_gha.py` writes corrupt or partial data. There is no stated pre-harvest backup, atomic-write strategy, or recovery procedure, leaving the snapshot store in an unspecified state on partial failure.
findings.location: scripts/harvest_gha.py; Approach section ('harvest the window as a seed AND persist coverage forward')
scenarios: Script crashes after writing 40 of 90 entries — the file is in an intermediate state with no documented recovery path
A bug in the coverage-line parser writes malformed JSON lines — downstream consumers silently fail or produce wrong metrics

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the dual-write overlap window: specify that the going-forward `test.yml` step should only append runs with timestamps/IDs *after* the harvest cutoff, OR that the harvest script skips runs already present in the store (linking to the idempotency fix above).
evidence: ABSENCE: no coordination mechanism between the harvest script and the new CI step is described
'harvest the window as a seed AND persist coverage forward' implies both paths are active simultaneously during initial rollout
findings.finding: The going-forward `test.yml` step and the one-time `harvest_gha.py` backfill can write to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` concurrently during the cutover window (e.g., a CI run completes while the harvest is in-flight), potentially writing duplicate entries for the same run ID.
findings.location: .github/workflows/test.yml step; Approach section ('persist coverage forward via the snapshot')
scenarios: A CI run completes and the new test.yml step appends its entry; the harvest script (running on the same day) also harvests that same run — the entry appears twice in the NDJSON file

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the isolated-adapter pattern and portability contract (core never imports CI adapters) in an ADR or AGENTS.md entry so future contributors understand and preserve it.
evidence: Plan states 'the CI source is an isolated adapter the core command never imports' and 'a parallel adapter could target another CI' — a novel pattern with an enforced contract
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry anywhere in the plan
The portability guard is enforced by a test, but the rationale and pattern are not documented for future contributors
findings.finding: The novel isolated-adapter pattern and its portability contract (core never imports the GHA adapter) are not captured in any ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc, leaving future contributors without guidance on the architectural rule.
findings.location: Approach section / AC portability guard line
scenarios: A future contributor adds a convenience import of the GHA adapter from core, breaking portability without understanding why the guard exists
A new CI adapter author doesn't know what interface/contract to implement
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR or a section in AGENTS.md: 'CI adapters live under metrics/adapters/ and are never imported by core commands; the contract is enforced by the portability-guard unit test. To add a new CI source, implement a parallel adapter module.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
